steps.title: Rheumatology
steps.text: Multiple Choice for Tertiary Care

steps.title: Septic Arthritis
steps.text: What is the initial management for pain in a child with septic arthritis at tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Pain management in septic arthritis includes paracetamol, NSAIDs (if available), and sometimes opiates for severe pain.

steps.title: Septic Arthritis
steps.text: What is the purpose of joint rest in managing septic arthritis?
steps.rationale: Resting the joint (e.g., using a sling for the arm or bed rest for the leg) helps reduce inflammation and supports recovery.

textChoices.text: To reduce inflammation and aid healing

steps.title: Septic Arthritis
steps.text: Which intravenous antibiotics are recommended for neonates with septic arthritis?
steps.rationale: Flucloxacillin and gentamicin are recommended IV antibiotics for neonates until fever subsides, followed by 4 weeks of oral therapy based on sensitivities.

steps.title: Septic Arthritis
steps.text: What is the recommended antibiotic and dose for older children with septic arthritis?
steps.rationale: Older children are treated with ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV once daily until fever settles, then switched to oral ciprofloxacin for up to 2 weeks if improving.

steps.title: Septic Arthritis
steps.text: What surgical intervention is included in tertiary care for septic arthritis?
steps.rationale: Orthopedic surgery may be needed to aspirate the joint, perform debridement, and inject sterile fluid until the infection clears.

textChoices.text: Surgical debridement, aspiration, and sterile fluid injection

steps.title: Septic Arthritis
steps.text: What factors influence the choice of antibiotics in the tertiary management of septic arthritis?
steps.rationale: Antibiotic choice in septic arthritis should be individualized based on several factors including age, predisposing conditions, suspected pathogens, culture results, and local antimicrobial resistance trends.

textChoices.text: The child’s weight and allergy history only
Predisposing factors, age and suspected organism
The presence of joint effusion only

steps.title: Osteomyelitis
steps.text: What is the recommended initial approach to manage pain in a child with osteomyelitis at the tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Tertiary care includes managing pain with paracetamol, NSAIDs (if available), and opiates when needed.

steps.title: Osteomyelitis
steps.text: What is the initial antibiotic regimen for a neonate with osteomyelitis?
steps.rationale: Flucloxacillin and gentamicin are the recommended IV antibiotics for neonates until fever settles, followed by 4 weeks of oral antibiotics based on susceptibility.

steps.title: Osteomyelitis
steps.text: What antibiotic and dose are recommended for older children with osteomyelitis?
steps.rationale: Older children should receive ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg IV daily, followed by oral ciprofloxacin for up to 4 weeks if fever resolves and condition improves.

steps.title: Osteomyelitis
steps.text: What is an important surgical step in the management of osteomyelitis at tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Osteomyelitis with abscess formation requires surgical drainage, which should be discussed with orthopedic surgeons.

steps.title: Osteomyelitis
steps.text: According to the note in the guideline, what should guide the choice of antibiotics in osteomyelitis?
steps.rationale: Antibiotic selection should be based on multiple factors including age, suspected organism, culture sensitivity, and local resistance patterns.

textChoices.text: Age, predisposing factors, culture results, and local resistance patterns

steps.title: Osteomyelitis
steps.text: What is the goal of physiotherapy and early mobilization after surgery for osteomyelitis?
steps.rationale: Postoperative care involves physiotherapy and early mobilization to maintain joint mobility and function once pain subsides.

textChoices.text: Prevent stiffness and preserve function when pain free

steps.title: Juvenile Dermatomyositis
steps.text: What is the initial corticosteroid used in first-line treatment of JDM?
steps.rationale: Methylprednisolone is used IV for 3 days at 10–30 mg/kg/day in first-line management.

steps.title: Juvenile Dermatomyositis
steps.text: How is methotrexate preferably administered in first-line JDM treatment?
steps.rationale: Methotrexate is preferably given subcutaneously at 15 mg/m² in JDM for better bioavailability and tolerance.

steps.title: Juvenile Dermatomyositis
steps.text: Why is TB prophylaxis included in the first-line management of JDM?
steps.rationale: Methotrexate is immunosuppressive and may reactivate latent TB, so TB INH prophylaxis is recommended when needed.

textChoices.text: All JDM patients are exposed to TB
Because methotrexate can increase TB reactivation risk

steps.title: Juvenile Dermatomyositis
steps.text: What is the correct dose range for daily hydroxychloroquine in JDM management?
steps.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is used at a dose of 3–6 mg/kg/day to manage skin and joint manifestations.

steps.title: Juvenile Dermatomyositis
steps.text: Which of the following is a second-line immunosuppressive agent for JDM?
steps.rationale: Azathioprine is listed under second-line pharmacologic agents for JDM when first-line therapy is insufficient.

steps.title: Juvenile Dermatomyositis
steps.text: Which treatment in JDM provides immunoglobulin replacement and modulation of immune response?
steps.rationale: IV immunoglobulins are used in second-line therapy for their immunomodulatory effects.

steps.title: Juvenile Dermatomyositis
steps.text: Which third-line agent in JDM is a B-cell depleting monoclonal antibody?
steps.rationale: Rituximab is a monoclonal antibody targeting CD20 on B cells and is used in refractory cases.

steps.title: Juvenile Dermatomyositis
steps.text: What is the mechanism of action of MMF (Mycophenolate mofetil) in JDM treatment?
steps.rationale: MMF suppresses lymphocyte proliferation by inhibiting purine synthesis and is used as third-line therapy.

textChoices.text: Inhibition of T and B cell proliferation

steps.title: Juvenile Dermatomyositis
steps.text: Which drug is classified as a calcineurin inhibitor and used as third-line treatment for JDM?
steps.rationale: Tacrolimus is a calcineurin inhibitor that modulates T-cell activation and is used in resistant JDM.

steps.title: Juvenile Dermatomyositis
steps.text: In third-line JDM therapy, which class of agents directly targets TNF-α?
steps.rationale: Anti-TNF agents are used in refractory cases of JDM that do not respond to other therapies.

steps.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
steps.text: What daily medication is recommended for all patients with SLE at tertiary level?
steps.rationale: Hydroxychloroquine is a cornerstone therapy for all SLE patients due to its role in reducing flares and improving survival.

steps.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
steps.text: Which corticosteroid regimen is commonly used in tertiary care for SLE?
steps.rationale: In cases of active or severe SLE, IV methylprednisolone is used initially, followed by a tapering course of oral prednisolone.

steps.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
steps.text: Which of the following is classified as a biological therapy used in SLE management?
steps.rationale: Rituximab is a biological agent used in refractory or severe SLE, particularly in patients not responding to conventional therapy.

steps.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
steps.text: What is the purpose of Vitamin D and calcium supplementation in SLE patients?
steps.rationale: Long-term steroid use can lead to osteoporosis, so Vitamin D and calcium are essential preventive supplements.

steps.title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus (SLE)
steps.text: What laboratory markers should be regularly monitored in tertiary care for SLE patients?
steps.rationale: Monitoring inflammatory markers (like ESR/CRP) and renal function (e.g., creatinine, urinalysis) is critical in managing disease activity and complications in SLE.

steps.title: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis
steps.text: What is the recommended disease-modifying drug if oligoarticular JIA is still active after 3 months of initial therapy?
steps.rationale: Methotrexate (10–15 mg/m²/week) is the standard second-line treatment if the disease remains active after 3 months of NSAIDs or intra-articular steroids.

steps.title: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis
steps.text: Which of the following should be monitored monthly during methotrexate therapy for oligoarticular JIA?
steps.rationale: FBC, LFTs, and creatinine should be monitored monthly for the first 3 months to detect methotrexate-related toxicity.

textChoices.text: Full blood count, liver enzymes, and creatinine

steps.title: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis
steps.text: What is the next step if there is no improvement after a month of initial therapy in polyarticular JIA?
steps.rationale: Methotrexate should be added if there is no improvement within one month of initial therapy.

steps.title: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis
steps.text: Which corticosteroid regimen is recommended for rapid symptom relief in polyarticular JIA?
steps.rationale: A starting dose of 1 mg/kg/day of prednisolone is used, then reduced to 5–7.5 mg/day if good response is achieved.

steps.title: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis
steps.text: In mild SJIA, what is the first-line treatment approach?
steps.rationale: For mild SJIA, prednisolone at 2 mg/kg/day is used with tapering once remission is achieved.

steps.title: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis
steps.text: What therapy is recommended as initial treatment for critically ill SJIA patients with serositis?
steps.rationale: High-dose IV methylprednisolone (30mg/kg for 3 days) is used for critically ill SJIA patients with serositis.

steps.title: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis
steps.text: If there is no improvement in psoriatic JIA after NSAID therapy, what is the next recommended treatment step?
steps.rationale: Anti-IL-17 biologic agents like secukinumab are recommended for patients not responding to first-line therapy.

steps.title: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis
steps.text: What is the initial management strategy for psoriatic JIA with no joint contractures and low disease activity?
steps.rationale: NSAIDs (like ibuprofen 10 mg/kg/does) are the initial treatment if the disease is mild and no joint contractures are present.

steps.title: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis
steps.text: What is the first-line therapy for enthesitis-related arthritis in primary care?
steps.rationale: Enthesitis-related arthritis is initially managed in primary care with NSAIDs to reduce inflammation and pain.

steps.title: Juvenile Idiopathic Arthritis
steps.text: What is the next step in severe enthesitis-related arthritis unresponsive to NSAIDs?
steps.rationale: Severe cases require corticosteroids (2mg /kg/day) and urgent referral to a pediatric rheumatologist.

steps.title: Kawasaki Disease
steps.text: What is the recommended dose and timing for IV immunoglobulin (IVIG) in Kawasaki disease?
steps.rationale: IVIG should be given at 2 g/kg over 10–12 hours, preferably within 10 days of illness onset to reduce risk of coronary artery complications.

textChoices.text: 2 g/kg in divided doses over 2 days

steps.title: Kawasaki Disease
steps.text: What is the anti-inflammatory dose of oral aspirin recommended during the acute phase of Kawasaki disease?
steps.rationale: During the acute febrile phase, aspirin is given at an anti-inflammatory dose of 30–50 mg/kg/day in 3 doses until the patient is afebrile for 2–3 days or until day 14.

steps.title: Kawasaki Disease
steps.text: What is the recommended maintenance (anti-platelet) dose of aspirin following the acute phase of Kawasaki disease?
steps.rationale: Aspirin is continued at 3–5 mg/kg/day as an anti-platelet agent for 6–8 weeks or until inflammatory markers normalize.

steps.title: Kawasaki Disease
steps.text: When should low-dose aspirin be continued indefinitely in Kawasaki disease?
steps.rationale: Aspirin is continued long-term if a coronary artery aneurysm is present to prevent thrombosis.

steps.title: Kawasaki Disease
steps.text: What defines Kawasaki Disease as not responding to primary treatment?
steps.rationale: Persistent or recurrent fever ≥36 hours after the initial IVIG indicates treatment failure.

textChoices.text: Temperature spike within 12 hours of IVIG
Fever ≥36 hours after initial IVIG dose

steps.title: Kawasaki Disease
steps.text: What is the recommended intervention if Kawasaki Disease does not respond to the initial IVIG dose?
steps.rationale: A second dose of IVIG (2 g/kg) over 10–12 hours is advised for non-responders to initial treatment.
